lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- Comparing & Choosing -- Comparatives, Superlatives & as...as
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Sandra Hayasaki | Aula 7 -- Comparing & Choosing

listenings.text: Last month I needed a new phone, and choosing one was harder than I expected. There were so many options. The first phone was cheaper, but the camera was worse and the battery did not last as long. The second one was more expensive, but the quality was much better. In the end I asked myself one simple question: which one is worth the money for me? I do not take many photos, so the best camera was not the most important thing. I prefer something simple and reliable. So I chose the cheaper phone, and honestly, it was the smartest choice I made all year. The lesson is clear: the most expensive option is not always the best one.

Last year my family had to choose between two very different trips. The beach was closer and a lot cheaper, but the mountains were quieter and far more beautiful. My husband preferred the beach because it was easier with the children. I liked the mountains because the air was fresher and the views were stunning. The two options were similar in price once we added the flights, so price was not the real difference. In the end we asked the most important question: which trip is more valuable for our family right now? We wanted rest, not adventure, so we chose the beach. It was not the most exciting choice, but it was the right one. Sometimes the simplest option is the best.

extra_audio.text: First, name the two options you are comparing. Then describe how they are similar. Next, point out the most important difference between them. After that, say which one you prefer and why. Finally, decide which option is worth it and make your choice.
